---
title: "3a Activity: Quarto Documents and Citations"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    link-external-newwindow: true
    css: _materials.css
    include-after-body: _progress.html
execute:
  enabled: false
---


## Goal

We will add Quarto to the same practice repository, create a small Quarto website project, write a document called "An Example Manuscript," add citations with BibTeX, render HTML and PDF outputs, and commit the source files.

Note: The checkboxes on this page are only stored locally in your browser. They are not submitted or synced anywhere.

::: {.guide-progress}

Progress: <span data-progress-count>0</span> / <span data-progress-total>0</span> steps complete.

<button class="reset-progress" type="button" data-progress-reset>Reset progress</button>

:::


## Activity

::: {.panel-tabset}

## 1. Add Quarto

Open the `python_tmr_practice` repository in Codespaces.
This should be the same repository you used for the Git, devcontainer, and `pyproject.toml` activities.

Open the Command Palette with **F1** or **Shift+Command+P**.

Search for **codespaces** and choose **Codespaces: Add Dev Container Configuration Files...**.

When VS Code asks what to do, choose **Modify your active configuration...**.

Search for **quarto** and choose the Quarto CLI feature:

```text
ghcr.io/rocker-org/devcontainer-features/quarto-cli
```

Accept the default or latest version.

The GUI will edit `.devcontainer/devcontainer.json` for you.
The Quarto CLI feature also asks VS Code to install the Quarto extension inside the devcontainer.

If VS Code asks to rebuild the container, follow that prompt. You can also open the Command Palette and choose **Codespaces: Rebuild Container**.

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3a-open-practice-repo">
  <span>I opened the practice repository in Codespaces.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3a-add-quarto-feature">
  <span>I added the Quarto devcontainer feature.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3a-quarto-version-works">
  <span>I rebuilt or reopened the Codespace after adding the feature.</span>
</label>

:::


## 2. Create Project Files

At the top level of the repository, create a file named `_quarto.yml`.

Paste this into the file:

```yaml
project:
  type: website
  output-dir: _site
  render:
    - index.qmd
    - paper.qmd

website:
  title: "Python TMR Practice"
  navbar:
    left:
      - text: "Home"
        href: index.qmd
      - text: "Paper"
        href: paper.qmd

bibliography: references.bib
csl: https://www.zotero.org/styles/academy-of-management-review

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
```

This file says that our repository contains a small Quarto website project.
It also says that the project will use `references.bib` for citations and an Academy of Management CSL style from the Zotero Style Repository.

Open `.gitignore`.

Add this line if it is not already there:

```text
_site/
```

The `_site/` folder will hold rendered website files.
For now, we want Git to track the source files, not the generated website folder.

At the top level of the repository, create a file named `index.qmd`.

Paste this into the file:

```markdown
---
title: "Python TMR Practice"
---

This site collects research outputs from this practice repository.

## Outputs

- [An Example Manuscript](paper.qmd)
```

Save the file.

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3a-create-quarto-yml">
  <span>I created `_quarto.yml`.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3a-ignore-site">
  <span>I added `_site/` to `.gitignore` or confirmed that it was already there.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3a-create-index">
  <span>I created `index.qmd`.</span>
</label>

:::


## 3. Create `references.bib`

At the top level of the repository, create a file named `references.bib`.

Paste these starter references into the file:

```bibtex
@article{boivie_2016,
  author = {Boivie, Steven and Graffin, Scott D. and Oliver, Abbie G. and Withers, Michael C.},
  title = {Come Aboard! Exploring the Effects of Directorships in the Executive Labor Market},
  journal = {Academy of Management Journal},
  volume = {59},
  number = {5},
  pages = {1681--1706},
  year = {2016},
  doi = {10.5465/amj.2014.0840}
}

@article{kiley_2023,
  author = {Kiley, Jason and McKenny, Aaron and Short, Jeremy and Smith, Anne},
  title = {Call for Papers for a Feature Topic: Having A Way with Words: Innovations and Improvements in Text Analysis Methods},
  journal = {Organizational Research Methods},
  volume = {26},
  number = {4},
  pages = {752--755},
  year = {2023},
  doi = {10.1177/10944281231195704}
}
```

Each reference starts with a cite key, such as `boivie_2016`.
That key is what we will use in the manuscript.

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3a-create-references-bib">
  <span>I created `references.bib` with the starter references.</span>
</label>

:::


## 4. Create The Manuscript

At the top level of the repository, create a file named `paper.qmd`.

Paste this into the file:

````markdown
---
title: "An Example Manuscript"
author: "Your Name"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: true
  typst:
    toc: true
    citeproc: true
  docx: default
---

## Introduction

This document is a small example of a research manuscript written in Quarto.

We can cite research from our field using cite keys from `references.bib`.
For example, prior work examines how directorships can matter in the executive labor market [@boivie_2016].
We can also cite work on text analysis methods in organizational research [@kiley_2023].

## A Small Code Example

Quarto documents can include executable Python code.

```{python}
import polars as pl

articles = pl.DataFrame(
    {
        "publisher": ["AOM", "SAGE"],
        "starter_references": [1, 1],
    }
)

articles
```

## References
````

Save the file.

With `paper.qmd` open, look at the bottom of the VS Code window.
You should see Quarto in the status bar.

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3a-create-manuscript">
  <span>I created `paper.qmd`.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3a-see-quarto-status">
  <span>I saw Quarto in the VS Code status bar.</span>
</label>

:::


## 5. Render The Manuscript

In the terminal, render the manuscript:

```bash
quarto render paper.qmd
```

This should create an HTML version, a PDF version, and a Word document.
The PDF uses Quarto's Typst backend because the document uses `format: typst`.
The Word document is useful when coauthors need to use track changes.

Then render the whole project:

```bash
quarto render
```

Open the rendered website preview when VS Code offers it, or open `_site/index.html` from the file explorer.
The Word version should be at `_site/paper.docx`.

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3a-render-manuscript">
  <span>I rendered `paper.qmd`.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3a-render-project">
  <span>I rendered the Quarto project.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3a-open-output">
  <span>I opened the rendered output and found the reference list and Word file.</span>
</label>

:::


## 6. Add Another Reference

Choose one article to add to `references.bib`:

- [Journal of Management article from SAGE](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/01492063261416425)
- [Strategic Management Journal article from Wiley](https://sms.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/smj.3134)
- [Academy of Management Review article from AOM](https://journals.aom.org/doi/10.5465/amr.2020.0371)

On the article page, look for the citation tool.
Choose BibTeX.
If the page gives you an option like **direct import**, uncheck it so the BibTeX opens in the browser.
Then use **Command+A** and **Command+C** to copy the BibTeX, and paste it at the bottom of `references.bib`.

Add one sentence to `paper.qmd` that cites the new reference.
Render the manuscript again.

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3a-add-reference">
  <span>I added one more BibTeX reference.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3a-cite-new-reference">
  <span>I cited the new reference in `paper.qmd`.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3a-rerender-citations">
  <span>I re-rendered the manuscript and found the new reference.</span>
</label>

:::


## 7. Commit And Push

Use Source Control:

1. Review the changes.
1. Stage `.devcontainer/devcontainer.json`, because the GUI edited it when you added the Quarto feature.
1. Stage the Quarto source files: `_quarto.yml`, `index.qmd`, `paper.qmd`, `references.bib`, and `.gitignore`.
1. Commit with a message such as:

```text
Added Quarto manuscript files.
```

Push or sync the commit to GitHub.

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3a-review-quarto-diff">
  <span>I reviewed the Quarto project changes.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3a-commit-quarto-files">
  <span>I committed the Quarto source files.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3a-push-quarto-files">
  <span>I pushed or synced the commit to GitHub.</span>
</label>

:::

:::
